question: What evidence supports preemptive TEVAR for uncomplicated acute or subacute type B aortic dissection?
source_access_type: Original publisher PDF held in private local source workspace
source_version_or_date: The Annals of Thoracic Surgery. 2022;113:1073-1092, with appended 2023 erratum
relevant_sections: TEVAR vs OMT for Uncomplicated TBAD
Treatment Paradigm for Acute Uncomplicated TBAD
Morphologic Features Posing Higher Risk of Late Sequelae
Table 1. Morphologic Features Posing High Risk of Late Sequelae

supporting_fields.appended_erratum: The appended 2023 erratum corrects reference number 92 to reference number 140 in one sentence; it does not change the identity of the 2022 guideline

extracted_claims.claim_text: A stepwise evaluation and treatment approach is recommended for acute or subacute uncomplicated TBAD, including confirmation that malperfusion and other indications of complicated disease are absent.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: A stepwise approach to the evaluation and treatment of acute/subacute uncomplicated TBAD should be applied that includes identification of the primary entry tear site location, defining the proximity and distance of the dissection to the LSA, calibration of the maximum orthogonal aortic diameter, and confirmation of the lack of any organ malperfusion or other indications of complicated disease.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: First recommendation under Uncomplicated TBAD; COR I; LOE B-NR.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Optimal medical therapy is the recommended treatment for uncomplicated TBAD.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: OMT is the recommended treatment for patients with uncomplicated TBAD.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Second recommendation under Uncomplicated TBAD; COR I; LOE B-NR.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Prophylactic TEVAR may be considered in uncomplicated TBAD to reduce late aortic-related adverse events and aortic-related death.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Prophylactic TEVAR may be considered in patients with uncomplicated TBAD to reduce late aortic-related adverse events and aortic-related death.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Third recommendation under Uncomplicated TBAD; COR IIB; LOE B-NR. The recommendation says may be considered and does not recommend routine TEVAR for every uncomplicated TBAD.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Close clinical follow-up after discharge is recommended for patients presenting with acute TBAD.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Close clinical follow-up after hospital discharge is recommended for patients presenting with acute TBAD.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Fourth recommendation under Uncomplicated TBAD; COR I; LOE B-NR.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline defines hyperacute, acute, subacute, and chronic intervals and separately categorizes TBAD as complicated, high risk, or uncomplicated.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Aortic dissections are now classified based on time from onset of symptoms as hyperacute (<24 hours), acute (1-14 days), subacute (15-90 days), and chronic (>90 days). TBADs are further categorized as 'complicated,' 'with high-risk features,' or 'uncomplicated.'
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Timing and clinical-risk categories are separate axes. Recommendation C01 explicitly applies to acute/subacute uncomplicated TBAD; the prophylactic-TEVAR recommendation C03 does not print a separate timing qualifier.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline distinguishes high-risk TBAD from uncomplicated TBAD using clinical and imaging features.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: High-risk features may include refractory pain, refractory hypertension, bloody pleural effusion, aortic diameter >40 mm, imaging evidence of malperfusion, entry tear on lesser curvature, or false lumen >22 mm. Uncomplicated TBADs do not have evidence of rupture, malperfusion syndrome, or high-risk features.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: The guideline treats high-risk TBAD as a category distinct from uncomplicated TBAD. Specialist review should not relabel patients with these features as uncomplicated without reconciling this definition.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; interpretation pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: TEVAR is indicated for complicated hyperacute, acute, or subacute TBAD with rupture and/or malperfusion when anatomy is favorable.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: TEVAR is indicated for complicated hyperacute, acute, or subacute TBADs with rupture and/or malperfusion and favorable anatomy for TEVAR.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: First recommendation under Acute Complicated TBAD; COR I; LOE B-NR. Included only to prevent applying the stronger complicated-TBAD indication to prophylactic treatment of uncomplicated TBAD.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Table 1 lists morphologic features associated with high risk of late sequelae.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Primary entry tear at greater curve of distal arch; Short proximity of entry tear to left subclavian artery ostium; Initial aortic diameter ≥40 mm; Initial false lumen diameter ≥22 mm; Number/size of fenestrations between true and false lumen; Stent graft-induced new entry; Partial false lumen thrombosis.
extracted_claims.section: Morphologic Features Posing Higher Risk of Late Sequelae
extracted_claims.table: Table 1. Morphologic Features Posing High Risk of Late Sequelae
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Table 1 concerns morphologic predictors of late sequelae. It is related to selective early treatment but is not itself a separate COR/LOE recommendation.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; clinical application pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline's INSTEAD summary distinguishes favorable remodeling from the absence of a two-year mortality difference and notes that the later favorable outcomes were post hoc.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Although favorable aortic remodeling occurred in the TEVAR/OMT cohort (91.3%) compared with the OMT cohort (19.4%), there were no differences in the primary end point of all-cause mortality or aorta-related mortality at 2 years. At 5 years, TEVAR/OMT was associated with improved aorta-specific survival and delayed disease progression, although these outcome measures were established post hoc.
extracted_claims.section: TEVAR vs OMT for Uncomplicated TBAD. INSTEAD trial
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Guideline summary of INSTEAD and INSTEAD-XL. Remodeling must not be treated as proof of a mortality benefit; the underlying trial reports remain the primary sources for outcome details.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for guideline-text extraction; underlying trials remain the primary sources for numerical outcomes

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline's ADSORB summary describes induced remodeling but states that long-term outcome comparisons were still needed and had not been reported.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: The conclusion was that remodeling with thrombosis of the false lumen and reduction of its diameter was induced by stent grafting, but long-term outcome comparisons are needed. These longer-term end points have not yet been reported.
extracted_claims.section: TEVAR vs OMT for Uncomplicated TBAD. ADSORB trial
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Guideline summary of ADSORB. The stated remodeling result is not evidence of a long-term clinical-outcome benefit.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for guideline-text extraction; the underlying trial remains the primary source for numerical outcomes

extracted_claims.claim_text: The treatment paradigm retains OMT as standard care and limits preemptive TEVAR to stable patients with suitable anatomy and high-risk features.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: OMT is the current standard of care treatment for all patients with uncomplicated TBAD. A thorough morphologic analysis of the aortic imaging enables identification of patients with high-risk features who may benefit from early TEVAR in addition to OMT. Thus, in stable TBAD with suitable anatomy and high-risk features, preemptive TEVAR may be considered to improve late outcome.
extracted_claims.section: Treatment Paradigm for Acute Uncomplicated TBAD
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Explanatory text supporting selective rather than routine preemptive TEVAR. This paragraph does not print a separate COR/LOE designation beyond the formal recommendations on printed p. 1079.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline distinguishes the remodeling response to TEVAR in acute and subacute phases from the reduced responsiveness of chronic dissection.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: After the acute phase, the dissection flap stiffens, and the dissection transitions into its chronic phase. The stiff, scarred dissection flap renders the aorta less responsive to false lumen compression and true lumen expansion by subsequent TEVAR in the chronic phase, and thus, TEVAR may be less effective in imparting favorable remodeling, as has been observed in the acute and subacute phases.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: This statement concerns remodeling responsiveness across phases; it does not establish a comparative clinical-outcome benefit for acute or subacute TEVAR.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

guideline_recommendations.recommendation: A stepwise approach to the evaluation and treatment of acute/subacute uncomplicated TBAD should be applied that includes identification of the primary entry tear site location, defining the proximity and distance of the dissection to the LSA, calibration of the maximum orthogonal aortic diameter, and confirmation of the lack of any organ malperfusion or other indications of complicated disease.
guideline_recommendations.location: Printed p. 1079; PDF p. 7; Uncomplicated TBAD

guideline_recommendations.recommendation: OMT is the recommended treatment for patients with uncomplicated TBAD.
guideline_recommendations.location: Printed p. 1079; PDF p. 7; Uncomplicated TBAD

guideline_recommendations.recommendation: Prophylactic TEVAR may be considered in patients with uncomplicated TBAD to reduce late aortic-related adverse events and aortic-related death.
guideline_recommendations.location: Printed p. 1079; PDF p. 7; Uncomplicated TBAD

guideline_recommendations.recommendation: Close clinical follow-up after hospital discharge is recommended for patients presenting with acute TBAD.
guideline_recommendations.location: Printed p. 1079; PDF p. 7; Uncomplicated TBAD

guideline_recommendations.recommendation: TEVAR is indicated for complicated hyperacute, acute, or subacute TBADs with rupture and/or malperfusion and favorable anatomy for TEVAR.
guideline_recommendations.population: Complicated hyperacute, acute, or subacute TBAD with rupture and/or malperfusion and favorable anatomy
guideline_recommendations.location: Printed p. 1077; PDF p. 5; Acute Complicated TBAD

limitations: The Class IIB prophylactic-TEVAR recommendation states that TEVAR may be considered; it does not recommend routine TEVAR for every patient with uncomplicated TBAD.

The prophylactic-TEVAR recommendation does not print a separate acuity qualifier, while the stepwise recommendation explicitly specifies acute/subacute uncomplicated TBAD and the supporting INSTEAD population spans subacute and chronic phases.

The guideline defines TBAD with listed high-risk features as a category distinct from uncomplicated TBAD, while its treatment-paradigm text discusses stable patients with high-risk features; specialist interpretation is required to reconcile terminology when applying the recommendation.
The guideline summarizes INSTEAD, INSTEAD-XL, and ADSORB, but those publications remain the primary sources for numerical outcomes, population definitions, and risk-of-bias assessment.

Favorable remodeling is not proof of mortality benefit. The guideline reports no two-year all-cause or aorta-related mortality difference in INSTEAD and states that longer-term ADSORB outcome comparisons were still needed.

The appended 2023 erratum changes reference number 92 to reference number 140 in a sentence about retrograde type A dissection after TEVAR; it does not change the recommendation text extracted here or the identity of the 2022 guideline.
reviewer_notes: Original-source extraction only. All decisions remain Pending; no item is verified by a reviewer or suitable for generated answers. The complicated-TBAD recommendation is included only to prevent its stronger Class I indication from being misapplied to uncomplicated TBAD. No numerical outcome records were created from the guideline's secondary summaries of individual studies. The appended erratum was reviewed and accounted for.
